title: PATED: Prognostic-Assisted Treatment Effect Detection in Randomized Trials
text: properly randomized clinical trial, baseline covariates balanced treatment arms expectation. PATED (Prognostic-Assisted Treatment Effect Detection) exploits fact: jointly modelling primary endpoint together panel prognostic baseline covariates accounting joint covariance, estimated treatment effect primary endpoint can adjusted remove chance imbalance, shrinking standard error without introducing bias. pated() returns data frame one row per term. first two rows adjusted (method = "PATED") unadjusted (method = "Standard") estimates primary treatment effect, standard error p-value. remaining rows hold per-arm regression prognostic covariate treatment indicator (method = "Prognostic"), estimates, standard errors, p-values, estimated correlation primary endpoint. function report Wald statistic relative efficiency directly; simple derived quantities compute inside vignette via small helper. vignette walks eight publicly available randomized trial datasets distributed CRAN packages. example fits Cox model primary time--event endpoint together GLMs baseline covariates, sharing treatment indicator. Datasets bundle disparate cohorts originate observational studies deliberately excluded randomization assumption underlying PATED satisfied.

title: PATED: Prognostic-Assisted Treatment Effect Detection in Randomized Trials
text: Diagnosis delivered plot() method, converts row pated object Wald z-statistic (estimate / stderr) arranges forest-style chart: red point top PATED-adjusted z primary endpoint. blue point just unadjusted z endpoint. gray points per-arm z-statistics baseline covariate (covariate ~ arm), ordered estimated correlation primary outcome (strongest top). Dashed vertical lines mark z = 0 z = +/-1.96. Two things look : Baseline balance. randomization gray prognostic points scatter around zero drifting past +/-1.96 chance. systematic shift one side, many covariates outside +/-1.96 band, suggests imbalance worth investigating relying adjusted estimate. Power gain. PATED removes component primary effect explained chance baseline imbalance. red PATED point sits noticeably zero blue unadjusted point, prognostic panel shaved variance primary estimate; red blue nearly coincide, covariates added little.

title: PATED: Prognostic-Assisted Treatment Effect Detection in Randomized Trials
text: Relative efficiency (RE) PATED unadjusted analysis ratio sampling variances. compute two treatment-effect rows pated return: RE=(stderrStandardstderrPATED)2 \text{RE} = \left(\frac{\text{stderr}_\text{Standard}}{\text{stderr}_\text{PATED}}\right)^2 value larger 1 means PATED produced tighter standard error unadjusted analysis data.
code: library(multipleOutcomes) library(dplyr) library(survival) options(digits = 2)  # pated() requires a subject identifier; add one if the data lack it. add_pid <- function(df) {   df$pid <- paste0("s-", seq_len(nrow(df)))   df }  # Derive RE from the two primary-effect rows that pated() returns. relativeEfficiency <- function(obj){   adj   <- obj[obj$method == 'PATED',    'stderr']   unadj <- obj[obj$method == 'Standard', 'stderr']   (unadj / adj)^2 }  printObject <- function(obj){   message(gsub('_', '::', deparse(substitute(obj))))   message(paste0('Relative Efficiency: ',                  format(relativeEfficiency(obj), digits = 3)))   print(obj) }

title: PATED: Prognostic-Assisted Treatment Effect Detection in Randomized Trials
text: Grana et al. (2002) reported small randomized trial locoregional radioimmunotherapy patients high-grade glioma (37 patients total: 18 control, 19 radioimmunotherapy). Patients received standard therapy without adjuvant radioimmunotherapy using 90Y-biotin. endpoint overall survival; available baseline covariates age, sex, histology (glioblastoma multiforme vs. grade III astrocytoma).
code: data(glioma, package = 'coin')  coin_glioma <-   pated(     coxph_(Surv(time = time, event = event)   ~ group, data_index = 1),     glm_(age                ~ group, family = "gaussian", data_index = 1),     glm_(sex                ~ group, family = "binomial", data_index = 1),     glm_(I(histology == 'GBM') ~ group, family = "binomial", data_index = 1),     data = list(add_pid(glioma %>%                           mutate(group = ifelse(group == 'Control', 0, 1),                                  event = 1 * event)))   )  printObject(coin_glioma) #>                               term family estimate stderr  pvalue     method #> 1 Surv(time = time, event = event)  PATED   -1.423   0.35 5.6e-05      PATED #> 2 Surv(time = time, event = event)  coxph   -1.829   0.46 5.9e-05   Standard #> 3            I(histology == "GBM")    glm   -1.012   0.68 1.4e-01 Prognostic #> 4                              age    glm   -3.272   4.61 4.8e-01 Prognostic #> 5                              sex    glm    0.095   0.66 8.9e-01 Prognostic #>   corr #> 1   NA #> 2 1.00 #> 3 0.59 #> 4 0.33 #> 5 0.13 plot(coin_glioma)

what: ddI vs. ddC in advanced HIV: JM::aids.id
title: PATED: Prognostic-Assisted Treatment Effect Detection in Randomized Trials
text: Abrams et al. (1994, NEJM) randomized HIV-infected patients failed intolerant zidovudine (AZT) didanosine (ddI) zalcitabine (ddC). aids.id patient-level (one row per subject) form distributed JM package contains 467 subjects (237 ddC, 230 ddI). endpoint time death, baseline covariates used CD4 count, sex, prior opportunistic infection status, AZT history (failure vs. intolerance).
code: data(aids.id, package = 'JM')  JM_aids.id <-   pated(     coxph_(Surv(time = Time, event = death) ~ drug, data_index = 1),     glm_(CD4    ~ drug, family = "gaussian", data_index = 1),     glm_(gender ~ drug, family = "binomial", data_index = 1),     glm_(I(prevOI == 'AIDS')        ~ drug, family = "binomial", data_index = 1),     glm_(I(AZT  == 'intolerance')   ~ drug, family = "binomial", data_index = 1),     data = list(add_pid(aids.id %>%                           mutate(drug = ifelse(drug == 'ddC', 1, 0))))   )  printObject(JM_aids.id) #>                               term family estimate stderr pvalue     method #> 1 Surv(time = Time, event = death)  PATED   -0.247   0.13  0.059      PATED #> 2 Surv(time = Time, event = death)  coxph   -0.210   0.15  0.150   Standard #> 3                              CD4    glm   -0.213   0.44  0.624 Prognostic #> 4              I(prevOI == "AIDS")    glm    0.084   0.20  0.668 Prognostic #> 5          I(AZT == "intolerance")    glm   -0.080   0.19  0.676 Prognostic #> 6                           gender    glm   -0.016   0.31  0.959 Prognostic #>    corr #> 1    NA #> 2  1.00 #> 3 -0.40 #> 4  0.35 #> 5 -0.23 #> 6 -0.03 plot(JM_aids.id)

title: PATED: Prognostic-Assisted Treatment Effect Detection in Randomized Trials
text: German Breast Cancer Study Group 2 trial (Schumacher et al., 1994) randomized 686 women node-positive primary breast cancer (440 without hormonal therapy, 246 tamoxifen) 2x2 factorial adjuvant hormonal therapy (tamoxifen, horTh) chemotherapy duration. endpoint recurrence-free survival; baseline covariates include tumour size, number positive lymph nodes, progesterone receptor level, tumour grade.
code: data(GBSG2, package = 'pec')  pec_GBSG2 <-   pated(     coxph_(Surv(time = time, event = cens) ~ horTh, data_index = 1),     glm_(tsize    ~ horTh, family = "gaussian", data_index = 1),     glm_(pnodes   ~ horTh, family = "gaussian", data_index = 1),     glm_(progrec  ~ horTh, family = "gaussian", data_index = 1),     glm_(I(tgrade == 'I')   ~ horTh, family = "binomial", data_index = 1),     glm_(I(tgrade == 'II')  ~ horTh, family = "binomial", data_index = 1),     glm_(I(tgrade == 'III') ~ horTh, family = "binomial", data_index = 1),     data = list(add_pid(GBSG2 %>%                           mutate(horTh = ifelse(horTh == 'yes', 1, 0))))   )  printObject(pec_GBSG2) #>                              term family estimate stderr pvalue     method #> 1 Surv(time = time, event = cens)  PATED    -0.33   0.11 0.0039      PATED #> 2 Surv(time = time, event = cens)  coxph    -0.36   0.12 0.0034   Standard #> 3                          pnodes    glm     0.19   0.43 0.6641 Prognostic #> 4                         progrec    glm    22.29  17.83 0.2113 Prognostic #> 5                           tsize    glm    -0.82   1.13 0.4693 Prognostic #> 6                I(tgrade == "I")    glm     0.24   0.24 0.3302 Prognostic #> 7              I(tgrade == "III")    glm    -0.28   0.19 0.1469 Prognostic #> 8               I(tgrade == "II")    glm     0.11   0.17 0.5288 Prognostic #>     corr #> 1     NA #> 2  1.000 #> 3  0.326 #> 4 -0.187 #> 5  0.169 #> 6 -0.159 #> 7  0.123 #> 8  0.006 plot(pec_GBSG2)

title: PATED: Prognostic-Assisted Treatment Effect Detection in Randomized Trials
text: trial, printed table shows adjusted PATED estimate unadjusted Standard estimate treatment effect primary endpoint, standard error two-sided p-value, followed one row per prognostic covariate. relativeEfficiency() helper turns two standard errors single RE summary, plot() method converts every row Wald z-statistic visual diagnostic. Read two views together: spread gray prognostic z-values shows well randomization balanced covariates, gap blue (unadjusted) red (PATED) points illustrates variance reduction RE value quantifies. RE close 1 means baseline covariates carried little prognostic information beyond randomization already balanced; RE appreciably 1 indicates meaningful efficiency gain.

title: Joint Covariance and Treatment-Effect Tests for Multiple Outcomes

Various applications package, including PATED (Prognostic Variables Assisted Treatment Effect Detection), multiple comparison adjustment, conditional power illustrated.

title: Joint Covariance and Treatment-Effect Tests for Multiple Outcomes
text: Let’s get started analyzing real randomized trial data. data randomized clinical trial indomethacin prevent post-ERCP pancreatitis. binary endpoint post-ERCP pancreatitis (yes/). collected, reformatted shared Dr. Peter Higgins R package medicaldata. information, please refer data set indo_rct medicaldata, manuscript. use PATED method estimate marginal treatment effect post-ERCP pancreatitis, adjusting set risk factors PEP potential prognostic covariates. expert disease, simply follow description information covariates medicaldata. compare PATED unconditional logistic regression model estimating treatment effect. covariates, regression model covar ~ rx fitted compute slope coefficient b̂\widehat b. correlation b̂\widehat b β̂\widehat\beta, unadjusted estimate treatment effect (outcome ~ rx), computed column corr.  plot shows covariates well balanced two arms (.e. z-values grey points close zero). evident PATED produces treatment effect estimate (-0.7514) close one unconditional logistic regression model (-0.7051), indicating good property unbiasness. Meanwhile, slightly lower SE (0.247 v.s. 0.2528) significant p-value (0.0023 v.s. 0.0053) obtained PATED. comparison, adjust list covariates logistic regression estimate conditional log odds ratio treatment. exclude asa81 asa due missing data, prophystent, therastent pdstent due numerical instability. conditional treatment effect estimated -0.8011, SE 0.2634 p-value 0.0024. example, seems like using unconditional logistic regression PATED can underestimate treatment effect compared regression adjustment. However, always case practice. also see examples regression adjustment can underestimate treatment effect substantially. fact, comparison invalid estimating different parameters different definitions treatment effect. terms statistical power detecting treatment effect, comprehensive simulation studies show PATED always outperform unconditional logistic regression, comparable (better ) regression adjustment.
code: library(multipleOutcomes) library(dplyr)  options(digits = 4)  data(indo)  # jointCovariance() and pated() need a per-row subject identifier in # column `pid`. The trial data already has an integer `id` — we just # stamp it as a string under the conventional name. indo <- indo %>% mutate(pid = paste0("s-", id))  # When there's only one dataset, `data_index` defaults to 1 and the # `list()` wrap is auto-applied, so neither needs to be spelled out. fit <- pated(   glm_(outcome     ~ rx, family = "binomial"),    glm_(risk        ~ rx, family = "gaussian"), glm_(psphinc     ~ rx, family = "binomial"),   glm_(precut      ~ rx, family = "binomial"),   glm_(difcan      ~ rx, family = "binomial"),   glm_(amp         ~ rx, family = "binomial"),   glm_(paninj family = "binomial"),   glm_(therastent  ~ rx, family = "binomial"),   glm_(pdstent     ~ rx, family = "binomial"),    data = indo )  plot(fit) print(fit) #>           term family  estimate  stderr what: Rectal Indomethacin for Prevention of Post-ERCP Pancreatitis — indo
title: Rectal Indomethacin for Prevention of Post-ERCP Pancreatitis — indo
text: Patient-level data multi-center, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled 2-arm trial (n = 602) rectal indomethacin (100 mg) versus placebo prevent post-ERCP pancreatitis high-risk patients, reported Elmunzer, Higgins, et al. (2012) New England Journal Medicine. dataset originally collected, cleaned, reformatted, released public teaching research use Dr. Peter D. R. Higgins medicaldata R package indo_rct. version shipped redistributed support worked examples package. variable definitions follow medicaldata; note columns stored numeric (rather factor) copy. Users need authoritative copy accompanying documentation consult medicaldata.

title: Rectal Indomethacin for Prevention of Post-ERCP Pancreatitis — indo
text: data frame 602 observations following 33 variables: id Subject identifier (numeric); leading digit indicates center. Range 1001–4003. site Study site (factor, 4 levels): 1_UM = University Michigan, 2_IU = Indiana University, 3_UK = University Kentucky, 4_Case = Case Western Reserve University. age Age years (numeric), range 19–90. risk Risk score post-ERCP pancreatitis (numeric), range 1–5.5. gender Sex (factor): 1_female, 2_male. outcome Primary outcome: post-ERCP pancreatitis (numeric, 1 = yes, 0 = ). sod Sphincter Oddi dysfunction present (factor): 0_no, 1_yes. pep History prior post-ERCP pancreatitis (factor): 0_no, 1_yes. recpanc History recurrent pancreatitis (factor): 0_no, 1_yes. psphinc Pancreatic sphincterotomy performed (factor): 0_no, 1_yes. precut Sphincter pre-cut needed enter papilla (factor): 0_no, 1_yes. difcan Cannulation papilla difficult (factor): 0_no, 1_yes. pneudil Pneumatic dilation papilla performed (factor): 0_no, 1_yes. amp Ampullectomy performed (factor): 0_no, 1_yes. paninj Contrast injected pancreas (factor): 0_no, 1_yes. acinar Pancreas appeared acinarization imaging (factor): 0_no, 1_yes. brush Brushings taken pancreatic duct (factor): 0_no, 1_yes. asa81 Aspirin used 81 mg per day (factor 3 levels): 0_no, 1_yes, third level retained source coding. asa325 Aspirin used 325 mg per day (factor 3 levels): 0_no, 1_yes, third level retained source coding. asa Aspirin used dose (factor 3 levels): 0_no, 1_yes, third level retained source coding. prophystent Pancreatic duct stent placed per endoscopist judgment (factor): 0_no, 1_yes. therastent Pancreatic duct stent placed treat narrowing (factor): 0_no, 1_yes. pdstent Pancreatic duct stent placed reason (factor): 0_no, 1_yes. sodsom Sphincter Oddi manometry performed (factor): 0_no, 1_yes. bsphinc Biliary sphincterotomy performed (factor): 0_no, 1_yes. bstent Biliary stent placed relieve obstruction (factor): 0_no, 1_yes. chole Choledocholithiasis present (factor): 0_no, 1_yes. pbmal Biliary duct pancreatic malignancy found (factor): 0_no, 1_yes. train Trainee participated ERCP (factor): 0_no, 1_yes. status Patient status (factor): 0_inpatient, 1_outpatient. type Sphincter Oddi dysfunction type (factor): 0_no SOD, 1_type 1, 2_type 2, 3_type 3. rx Treatment assignment (numeric, 1 = indomethacin, 0 = placebo). bleed Reportable gastrointestinal bleeding (numeric, coded 1 = , 2 = yes; NA assessed).

title: Fitting Regression Models for Multiple Outcomes and Returning the Matrix of Covariance — jointCovariance
text: ... objects returned glm_(), coxph_(), logrank_(), gee_() gmm_(). data data frame models fitted dataset; otherwise list data frames fitting models .... Note dataset can used fit multiple models, thus, length(data) unnecessary equal number models .... row names data frame treated subject IDs. Instead, data frame consist column pid subject IDs. two records different data frames correspond subject, values pid consistent. data frames used GEE, pid defines clusters. See id Details section gee:gee. nboot non-zero integer bootstrap adopted. default 0. compute_cov logic. TRUE nboot > 0, empirical covariance matrix computed using bootstrap estimate returned. Bootstrap estimate abandoned. FALSE, bootstrap estimate returned empirical covariance matrix computed. seed random seed generate bootstrap data.

title: Fitting Regression Models for Multiple Outcomes and Returning the Matrix of Covariance — jointCovariance
code: ## More examples can be found in the vignettes. library(survival) library(mvtnorm) library(tidyr) genData <- function(seed = NULL){      set.seed(seed)   n <- 300   sigma <- matrix(.7, 4, 4)   diag(sigma) <- 1   v <- rmvnorm(n, sigma = sigma)   x1 <- v[, 1]   x2 <- v[, 2]   z1 <- (v[, 3] > 0) + 0   z2 <- v[, 4]      trt <- rbinom(n, 1, .5)      bet <- c(-.3,.3)   y <- -log(runif(n))/     exp(-.3 * x1 + .3 * x2 + z1 * .5 - z2 * .3 + .1 * trt + rnorm(n))      z1[sample.int(n, 50)] <- NA   z2[sample.int(n, 50)] <- NA   x1[sample.int(n, 50)] <- NA   x2[sample.int(n, 50)] <- NA   death <- ifelse(y > 2, 0, 1)   y[y > 2] <- 2      pid <- paste0('pid-', 1:n)   ret <- data.frame(     y = y, trt = trt,      z1 = z1, z2 = z2,      x1 = x1, x2 = x2,      death, pid)   ret }  dat1 <- genData()  ## create a dataset with repeated measurements x dat2 <- dat1 %>% pivot_longer(c(x1, x2), names_to='visit', values_to='x') %>%    dplyr::select(x, trt, visit, pid) %>% as.data.frame()  dat2$visit <- as.factor(dat2$visit) dat2$pid <- as.factor(dat2$pid)  fit <- jointCovariance(   coxph_(Surv(time = y, event = death) ~ trt, data_index = 1),   logrank_(Surv(time = y, event=death) ~ trt, data_index = You can compute the covariance matrix using $bootstrap_estimate.   qfit <- jointCovariance(   quantile_(y ~ trt, probs = c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75)),   glm_(z2 ~ trt, family = 'gaussian'),   data = dat1, nboot = 30, seed = 1) # }

title: Prognostic Variables Assisted Treatment Effect Detection — pated
code: ## More examples can be found in the vignettes. library(survival) library(mvtnorm)  genData <- function(seed = NULL){   set.seed(seed)   n <- 300   sigma <- matrix(c(1, .6, .6, 1), 2)   x <- rmvnorm(n, sigma = sigma)   z1 <- rbinom(n, 1, .6)   z2 <- rnorm(n)   trt <- rbinom(n, 1, .5)    bet <- c(-.2, .2)   y <- -.5 + x %*% bet + z1 * .3 - z2 * .1 + .1 * trt - .1 * rnorm(n)   death <- rbinom(n, 1, .8)   data.frame(     y = as.numeric(y), trt = trt,     z1 = z1, z2 = z2,     x1 = x[, 1], x2 = x[, 2],     death, pid = paste0('s-', seq_len(n))   ) }  dat <- genData(seed = 31415926)  ## `data_index` defaults to 1 in every spec constructor and a single ## data.frame is auto-wrapped into a list, so neither needs spelling out ## when all models are fitted on the same dataset. fit <-   pated(     coxph_(Surv(time = y, event = death) ~ trt),     glm_(z1 ~ trt, family = 'binomial'),     glm_(z2 ~ trt, family = 'gaussian'),     glm_(x1 ~ trt, family = 'gaussian'),     glm_(x2 ~ trt, family = 'gaussian'),     data = dat   )  fit #>                            term family     estimate     stderr       pvalue #> 1 Surv(time = y, event = death)  PATED -0.288684461 0.08056904 0.0003395805 #> 2 Surv(time = y, event = death)  coxph -0.201743178 0.13062378 0.1224769862 #> 3                            z1    glm -0.269021294 0.23351160 0.2492930398 #> 4                            z2    glm  0.110597580 0.11184325 0.3227304829 #> 5                            x2    glm  0.039205611 0.11165593 0.7254917405 #> 6                            x1    glm  0.007879826 0.11228370 0.9440521271 #>       method       corr #> 1      PATED         NA #> 2   Standard  1.0000000 #> 3 Prognostic -0.4589612 #> 4 Prognostic  0.4075322 #> 5 Prognostic -0.3087809 #> 6 Prognostic  0.2148699

title: multipleOutcomes 0.17.1
text: netbenefit_() now treats NA endpoint column “tied level, fall ” rather erroring deep U-statistic loop, warns per fit listing affected columns.

title: multipleOutcomes 0.17.0
text: netbenefit_() adapter hierarchical net-benefit (win-difference) statistic, endpoints built nb_tte(), nb_continuous(), nb_binary() priority encoded list order.

title: multipleOutcomes 0.16.4
text: New test-inplace-formula.R verifies -place formula transformations ((z > 4), log(x), scale(x), factor(x), Surv(t / c, 1 - cnsr), multi-variable Boolean expressions, LHS arithmetic) produce numerically identical PATED output pre-computed-column equivalents, across coxph_() primaries glm_() prognostic specs.

title: multipleOutcomes 0.16
text: multipleOutcomes() legacy *MO() wrappers (coxphMO, glmMO, geeMO, logrankMO, kmMO, quantileMO) removed. package’s main entry point now jointCovariance(), component model specified constructor: glm_(), coxph_(), logrank_(), gee_(), mmrm_(), km_(), quantile_(). pated() accepts spec constructors via .... Datasets supplied jointCovariance() / pated() must now contain column pid carrying subject identifiers. Records pid across different data frames refer subject.

title: multipleOutcomes 0.16
text: mmrm_() adapter mixed models repeated measures. km_() adapter Kaplan-Meier survival probabilities (bootstrap-, since empirical S(t) closed-form score). quantile_() adapter -arm quantile differences (bootstrap-). gee_() adapter exposing GEE fit’s per-cluster score Hessian slots joint asymptotic covariance machinery alongside engines. conf_type = "arcsin" fully supported km_() (previously silently fell back logit transformation).

title: multipleOutcomes 0.16
text: data_index defaults 1 every spec constructor, single data frame auto-wrapped, jointCovariance(spec1(...), spec2(...), data = my_df) shortest valid call. Spec constructors validate data_index (must positive integer scalar); jointCovariance() adds upfront bounds check naming offending spec data_index exceeds number supplied data frames. pated() emits warning residual variance goes negative — typically sign prognostic variable collinear primary outcome another prognostic.

title: multipleOutcomes 0.16
text: KMAdapter$fit_model() longer strips names self$estimate, id_map entries KM models now carry time_(strata)_(time) labels pated()’s arm lookup relies . parseTreatmentVariableFromCall() now walks formula AST instead regex-parsing deparsed call, formulas nested parentheses (e.g., y ~ arm + us(visit | pid) mmrm_) parse correctly. fitKMCurve() uses survival::summary(..., extend = TRUE) consistently, preventing NAs leaking bootstrap covariance matrix resample’s stratum observations past requested time. pated() longer relies .null(family), silently bound stats::family (function) family argument removed.

title: multipleOutcomes 0.16
text: New testthat suite (52 tests / 245 expectations) covering every engine jointCovariance() pated(). Tests split fast tier (~4 s) Monte Carlo tier (~25 s, opt-via MULTIPLEOUTCOMES_RUN_MC=1) validates empirical vs. theoretical covariance single-engine, cross-engine, partial-overlap, kitchen-sink configurations. Regression fixture README example pinned 1e-10 (file inst/testdata/readme_pated_reference.rds). GitHub Actions workflows code coverage (codecov) pkgdown. README example, vignette, help-page examples migrated new spec-constructor API.

title: multipleOutcomes 0.15
text: Initial support Kaplan-Meier new spec-constructor interface via km_(). pated() extended handle KM time-stratum parameter vectors, including transformed-S(t) point estimates, pointwise confidence intervals, KM-vs-PATED curve comparison plots.
